main_ingredients: fish head (typically red snapper or salmon)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of fish head curry is approximately 350 g, comprising the fish head portion with curry sauce and vegetables, usually shared among two to four diners.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Head Curry — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for South East Asia; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central analogous fish curry values
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) / FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Fish head curry nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD fish curry entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA fish curry data. The dish is a braised fish head in spiced coconut-milk curry sauce. Values represent the composite dish including sauce and vegetables. Core macros cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for fish curry (SR Legacy) to confirm plausibility. Per-100g values reflect the mixed dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fish curry dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for curry-based fish dishes and coconut-milk curry preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fish head curry found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous coconut-milk fish curry entries in the database. The dish is low in carbohydrate (3.5 g per 100 g) and high in protein and fat, which suppresses glycemic response. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (35 × 3.5 × 3.50) / 100 = approximately 4.3 g, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 60–70% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (3.5 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~35)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fish head curry is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice; its low carbohydrate content and high protein contribute to a low glycemic load of approximately 4.3 per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~3.9 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from coconut milk and the high sodium content warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; however, the omega-3 fatty acids from the fish head provide a cardioprotective benefit that partially offsets the saturated fat concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Head Curry is a popular and iconic dish in Malaysian and Singaporean cuisine, believed to have originated in Singapore in the 1950s when an Indian chef adapted a South Indian fish curry recipe using a whole fish head at the request of a Chinese customer. The dish typically uses the head of a large fish — most commonly red snapper (ikan merah) or salmon — simmered in a richly spiced, tangy coconut-milk curry sauce. The sauce is built from a curry paste containing dried and fresh chillies, turmeric, lemongrass, galangal, and fenugreek, and is balanced with tamarind for sourness and coconut milk for creaminess. Vegetables such as okra (lady's fingers), eggplant (brinjal), and tomatoes are cooked in the curry alongside the fish head. The fish head itself is prized for its gelatinous cheek meat, the soft flesh around the jaw, and the collagen-rich skin, which give the dish a rich mouthfeel. The curry is moderately spicy, deeply aromatic, and intensely savoury. Nutritionally, fish head curry is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-GI dietary patterns. The fish provides omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin B12, and phosphorus. The coconut milk contributes saturated fat and medium-chain triglycerides. Sodium content is elevated due to the curry sauce and is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. It is typically served as a shared dish with steamed white rice or roti canai in Malaysian and Singaporean restaurants.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA fish curry entries, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central analogous fish curry data. No single approved source provides an exact match for fish head curry as a composite dish; values represent a well-grounded estimate for the mixed dish as served. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. The input record listed the food name as 'Fish Head'; the canonical name has been expanded to 'Fish Head Curry' to reflect the actual dish served at the referenced restaurant (Singapore Banana Leaf Restaurant — Circular Road), which is a South Indian-style fish head curry. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source databases for this dish category. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases for this dish. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases for this dish. 5. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero per IMR Malaysia MyFCD; fish and coconut-milk curry preparations do not contain industrially produced trans fats. 6. GI is an analogous estimate of 35 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for coconut-milk fish curry dishes; no direct measurement exists for fish head curry. 7. The cuisine tags in the input (Noodle, Fried Rice, Roti Prata) reflect the broader restaurant menu and are not applicable to this dish record. 8. Price data (SGD 25.00) is preserved from the field survey source for reference only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
